step_1_context.text: Some nouns are usually plural and take a plural verb:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
Some nouns are usually plural and take a plural verb:
 • The company's earnings have increased for the last five years.
step_2_explanation.formula: Although the words data and media (= newspaper, television, etc.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
Some uncountable nouns always end in -s and look as if they are plural, but when we use them as the subject they have a singular verb:
 • The news from the Middle East seems very encouraging.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 42.1.1 Dr Darsee's present whereabouts ___ unknown.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Whereabouts can be used with a singular or plural verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 42.1.2 Phenomena such as sun spots ___ puzzled scientists for centuries.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Phenomena' is the plural of 'phenomenon' and takes a plural verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 42.2.1 Modern linguistics ___ often said to have begun at the start of the 20th century.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Linguistics' refers to an academic subject, so it takes a singular verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 42.2.2 Contact us by whatever means ___ most convenient for you: phone, email or text.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: In this context ('phone, email or text'), 'means' refers to multiple methods, so it takes a plural verb.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 42.3.1 On average, 25 litres of water ___ (be) used each day per household.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: A phrase referring to an amount or quantity usually takes a singular verb.
